one_line_takeaway: FPBA combines frequency-domain spectrum perturbations with a lightweight 'post-train Bayesian' surrogate (appended MLP heads, no re-training) to produce highly transferable, imperceptible adversarial examples that break state-of-the-art AI-generated-image detectors across architectures, generators and compression, while showing adversarial training fails to defend them.
key_finding: FPBA achieves the highest white-box ASR (99.6% average, vs S2I 93.1%, PGD 93.1%, SSAH 97.7%, IFGSM/MIFGSM 66.2%) and, critically, the best black-box transfer (50.5% average, beating PGD by 8.4, S2I by 11.9, MIFGSM by 23.3, IFGSM by 25.7, and SSAH by 49.1 points; SSAH's 97.7% white-box success collapses to ~1.4% transfer). It also surpasses Deepfake-specific attacks (89.4% avg vs FakePolisher 77.9/70.9%) while producing far more imperceptible perturbations (MSE 16.08, PSNR 36.26 dB, SSIM 0.94 vs PGD 30.00/33.51/0.88). Two notable analysis findings: AIGI detectors exhibit gradient masking (MIFGSM performs no better than IFGSM until random initialization is added, which raises average ASR from 40.4% to 58.6%), and adversarial training (PGD-AT, TRADES) fails to converge on this task, collapsing clean accuracy to ~50% (random guessing) with near-0% robust accuracy, so it is not a usable defense. Attack transferability correlates positively with a detector's benign accuracy on a generator subset (poor on low-accuracy ADM/BigGAN subsets).
core_contribution: The paper proposes FPBA (Frequency-based Post-train Bayesian Attack), a transferable adversarial-example attack that makes AI-generated-image (AIGI) detectors misclassify fake images as real. Its two novel ingredients are the paper's OWN contribution (not cited work): (1) crafting perturbations in the frequency domain via random spectrum transformation (DCT by default) to push an image away from its ground-truth frequency distribution, motivated by the paper's spectrum-saliency-map observation that different detectors attend to different frequency components; and (2) a 'post-train Bayesian strategy' that turns a single frozen pre-trained surrogate into a Bayesian one by appending a tiny trainable MLP component (skip-connected to the original logits) rather than re-training an ensemble of surrogates, with appended weights sampled via Stochastic Gradient Adaptive HMC (K=3 appended models). A hybrid step combines spatial and frequency gradients. The stated goal is the first systematic study of the adversarial robustness of general AIGI detectors (as opposed to face-only Deepfake detectors) under both white-box and black-box transfer settings.
threat_model: Evasion attack against binary real/fake AIGI detectors. Adversary adds an L-infinity-bounded perturbation (budget epsilon=8/255 in the main experiments; also tested at 4/255 and 12/255) to a fake image so the detector outputs 'real'. Two access levels: white-box (attacker has the victim model) and, more importantly, black-box transfer (attacker only owns a surrogate detector, e.g. CNNSpot/ResNet or Swin-ViT, and transfers adversarial examples to unseen heterogeneous victims across CNNs, ViTs, frequency-, CLIP-, and diffusion-based detectors). The attack is also evaluated under real-world degradations (JPEG compression, Gaussian blur) and against adversarial-training defenses. The paper is offensive: it demonstrates the vulnerability and shows a standard defense (adversarial training) fails, rather than proposing a robust detector.
limitations: The attack is weakest against frequency-based detectors (top-2 average transfer 43.1%, actually 1.5 points BELOW PGD) because their gradients are sparse in the frequency domain, and against high-level-semantic CLIP-based UnivFD (only 16.6% ASR, with fake images oddly more robust than real). Transfer to ViT is consistently lower than to CNNs due to less-overlapping frequency-sensitive regions. Cross-generator transfer is poor on subsets where the detector itself has near-random accuracy (ADM, BigGAN), which the authors argue makes such evaluations uninformative. The work is purely offensive: it does not deliver a robust detector, only shows adversarial training does not work here, leaving robust AIGI detection an open problem. Some improvements are modest or non-monotonic (e.g. on the Synthetic LSUN/ProGAN MobileNet-surrogate block FPBA's 34.3% average is close to PGD's, and DWT slightly outperforms the chosen default DCT).

datasets_used: Synthetic LSUN (CNNSpot): 360k real LSUN images + 360k ProGAN-generated fakes

GenImage subset: 162k real ImageNet images + 162k Stable Diffusion v1.4 fakes (full GenImage = 1331k real / 1350k fake from 8 generators; cross-generator test uses Midjourney, SD v1.4/v1.5, ADM, Wukong, BigGAN)
Synthetic FFHQ (Deepfake): 50k real FFHQ faces + 50k StyleGAN2-generated faces
quantitative_results: White-box average ASR: FPBA 99.6% vs SSAH 97.7%, S2I 93.1%, PGD 93.1%, IFGSM 66.2%, MIFGSM 66.2%
Black-box transfer average ASR: FPBA 50.5%, exceeding PGD by 8.4, S2I by 11.9, MIFGSM by 23.3, IFGSM by 25.7, SSAH by 49.1 points
vs Deepfake attacks (Table III, fake-only ASR): FPBA 89.4% avg on both LSUN and GenImage; FakePolisher 77.9/70.9%, FaceAttacker 19.7/63.7%, TraceEvader 1.9/31.1%
Transfer to SOTA detectors from CNNSpot surrogate (Table V): FPBA 55.6% avg vs PGD 50.2%, S2I 43.9%, MIFGSM 26.6% (FreqMask 98.4%, GramNet 87.8%, UnivFD only 16.6%)
Diffusion reconstruction detectors, average precision AP (lower=stronger attack, Table IX): FPBA AEROBLADE 43.1 / DIRE 40.8 vs clean 80.0/100.0 and PGD 46.9/43.2
Imperceptibility (Table XIV): FPBA MSE 16.08, PSNR 36.26 dB, SSIM 0.94 vs PGD 30.00/33.51/0.88 and FakePolisher 34.63/32.92/0.88
Gradient masking: adding random initialization to MIFGSM raises average ASR from 40.4% to 58.6%
Adversarial-training defense (Table XI): PGD-AT/TRADES clean accuracy collapses to ~50%, robust accuracy near 0% (e.g. UnivFD TRADES 0% robust on ProGAN)
Domain ablation (Table XVI, CNNSpot surrogate, DenseNet/MobileNet/Spec/ViT): spatial-only 71.5/34.0/27.1/11.0%, frequency-only 91.0/64.7/36.7/16.9%, spatial-frequency (FPBA) 96.2/66.2/47.9/21.0%
Spectrum-transform count N ablation: N=0 gives 48.8% avg, N=5 (default) 69.2%, N=10 70.3% (diminishing returns beyond 5)
Compression robustness (Table XII, Swin-ViT, Blur+JPEG p=0.5): FPBA drops only 0.8 points (to 99.2%) vs S2I -19.1, IFGSM -12.2, PGD -12.0
